question: Which production process type produces unique, one-of-a-kind products specifically designed to customer requirements?
options: Continuous flow

Job shop (project manufacturing): each product is custom-designed and built from scratch based on the individual customer's specifications; examples include: custom home construction, bespoke tailoring, custom software development, shipbuilding, aircraft manufacturing; job shops have high flexibility, high skill requirements, and high cost per unit but can produce exactly what each customer needs
Mass production
Batch processing
explanation: Job shop / project: completely customized, single-unit production. Each 'job' moves through the process differently based on its unique requirements. Equipment is general-purpose (can make many different things). Labor is highly skilled. Examples: NASA spacecraft (each is unique), custom software projects, custom furniture makers, law firm legal cases. Trade-off: maximum flexibility, highest cost per unit, slowest throughput. The operations challenge: scheduling unique jobs through shared equipment when each job has different routing.

question: A bakery produces bread in batches of 100 loaves at a time, switching production to muffins after each bread run is complete. This is an example of which production process type?
options: Continuous flow

Batch processing: production occurs in discrete groups (batches) of the same product; after each batch completes, equipment is set up for the next product; batch processing enables some variety (bread then muffins then croissants) while maintaining more efficiency than pure job shop; trade-offs: changeover time between batches creates downtime; inventory accumulates (the batch must be stored until sold); used by: pharmaceutical manufacturers, food processors, printed materials, electronics assembly
Job shop
Mass customization
explanation: Batch process: positioned between job shop (fully custom) and mass production (fully continuous). Examples: pharmaceutical manufacturers (produce batch of Drug A, clean, produce batch of Drug B), craft breweries (batch of IPA, then batch of stout), print shops (print 500 brochures, then 200 posters). Key metrics: batch size (bigger batches = less changeover time per unit, but more inventory), changeover time (SMED techniques reduce changeover), and scheduling (which batches run in what sequence to minimize total cost). Beer: bottled in batches; Coke: continuous flow because no changeover needed.

question: An oil refinery operates 24 hours per day, 365 days per year, producing petroleum products through a continuous, uninterrupted process. This is an example of which process type?
options: Batch processing

Continuous flow (also called continuous process or process industry): production never stops; the same product flows through the facility continuously with no start/stop or changeover; characteristics: highly capital-intensive equipment, extremely high volume, very low cost per unit, essentially no product variety; used in: oil refining, chemical production, paper manufacturing, electricity generation, some food processing (orange juice); the process itself transforms inputs continuously — you can't 'stop' an oil refinery without significant safety and restart costs
Mass production
Job shop
explanation: Continuous flow: the most efficient process type for single-product, high-volume production. An oil refinery produces millions of barrels per day of the same product mix. Shutting down a continuous process is expensive and risky — refineries run 330+ days/year with planned maintenance shutdown periods. Other examples: steel mills (blast furnaces), cement plants, power generation plants, chemical facilities. The trade-off: maximum efficiency and lowest cost per unit, but essentially zero flexibility (the refinery can't suddenly produce cheese). Process type selection: what range of products must you make? If exactly one product in very high volume, continuous flow is optimal.

question: Toyota's production line makes thousands of different vehicle configurations (colors, trim levels, engines) in a single production facility. This is most accurately described as:
options: Job shop production since each vehicle has unique specifications
Mass production since the same process runs continuously

Mass customization (mixed-model assembly): the production system can efficiently produce customized products — each vehicle is uniquely configured by the customer's order but is assembled on the same line using the same processes; this requires: production sequence planning (group similar configurations to minimize changeover), supplier kanban delivering specific parts for each specific vehicle arriving at the right point, and highly standardized process steps that accommodate variety through modular components; Toyota's 'Production Smoothing' (Heijunka) sequences models to minimize disruption while producing to customer orders
Continuous flow since the line never stops
explanation: Mass customization (Joe Pine): the holy grail of operations — customize at the speed and cost of mass production. Toyota's Heijunka (production leveling) sequences mixed models: 1 Camry, 1 RAV4, 1 Corolla, 1 Camry, 1 RAV4 — not all Camrys then all RAV4s (which would be batch). Leveling absorbs model changeover across the day and allows a continuous-flow-like system to produce variety. Requirements: standardized subcomponents (all models use the same door hinge installation process), modular variety (different options use the same mounting points), and real-time information (each vehicle's build sheet travels with the car, triggering correct part deliveries). This is why Toyota can produce a car exactly to your specification in 10–14 days.

question: A law firm handles three types of matters: (1) litigation (long, complex, unpredictable), (2) routine contract review (standardized, predictable), (3) M&A transactions (large, complex, moderate predictability). What process design fits each matter type?
options: All legal work is inherently custom and should use identical job shop processes

Different matter types warrant different process designs: Litigation = project/job shop (each case is unique, unpredictable duration, requires senior attorney attention throughout); Routine contracts = batch or standardized workflow with templates and junior attorney review, perhaps automated contract analysis software (high volume, predictable, standardizable); M&A transactions = structured project management with phase gates (due diligence, regulatory, closing) but more standardized than litigation since M&A processes have repeatable phases; the insight: using expensive senior attorney time (job shop) on standardized contract reviews is an operations waste; matching process type to matter complexity reduces cost and increases throughput
Legal services cannot be standardized and must all use project-based processes
Technology can replace all legal process types regardless of complexity
explanation: Professional services process design: applying manufacturing process logic to service operations. The matrix: simple/predictable work → standardized processes (batch), complex/unique work → customized processes (job shop). Applying job shop (senior attorney, high cost, custom) to routine work is like using a custom machine shop to make paper clips. Law firms that haven't applied process type analysis to their work mix: (1) Overcharge on routine work (senior attorney billing for contract reviews); (2) Under-resource on complex work (too junior attorneys managing major litigation). Document review automation (AI) is a batch/continuous process replacing job shop legal work — reducing cost by 90% on the standardizable portion.

question: McDonald's french fries use a continuous process; Shake Shack's gourmet burgers use a batch/job shop hybrid; a 5-star restaurant's tasting menu uses a pure job shop. Compare the unit economics of each.
options: All three restaurant models have similar unit economics

Process type determines unit economics: McDonald's fries (continuous-like): frozen fries dropped in oil, 3.5-minute automated cook cycle, $0.20 cost per serving, served to thousands daily; Shake Shack burgers (batch): each burger assembled to order in ~3 minutes, fresh ingredients, higher quality, $2–3 cost per serving, moderate volume; 5-star tasting menu (job shop): each dish is hand-crafted by trained chefs, plated individually, 45+ minutes of preparation per dish, $40–80 cost per cover, perhaps 40 covers per night; the pattern: job shop = highest quality, highest cost, lowest volume; continuous = lowest quality ceiling, lowest cost, highest volume; neither model is 'better' — they serve different customer willingness to pay and market positions
Restaurant economics are identical regardless of production process type
Higher cost per unit always means higher quality in food service
explanation: Process type and unit economics matrix: Cost per unit inversely related to volume and standardization. McDonald's fries: COGS ~20% of $3 selling price = $0.60 cost. Shake Shack burger: COGS ~30% of $10 = $3 cost. Per Se (NYC) tasting menu: COGS ~35% of $365 = $128 cost. Each is profitable because price exceeds cost, but only at the correct volume for the process type. The restaurant industry failure pattern: attempting high-end restaurant quality (job shop process) at fast-food prices (continuous process economics) — the cost structure is incompatible with the price point. Understanding process type economics is essential for restaurant viability.

question: A semiconductor wafer fabrication facility (fab) like TSMC or Intel requires investments of $15–$20 billion and years to construct. Once built, the fab runs 24/7. What process type considerations drive these characteristics?
options: Semiconductor manufacturing costs are determined primarily by the size of the manufacturing workforce

Semiconductor fabs represent extreme capital intensity from process type constraints: (1) Continuous flow requirement — once fabrication begins on a wafer batch, it cannot stop (photolithography, etching, deposition processes run sequentially and cannot pause without destroying the wafer); (2) Environmental requirements — class-1 cleanrooms (fewer than 1 particle per cubic foot; vs. 35M in outside air) require elaborate sealed environments; (3) Equipment precision — EUV (extreme ultraviolet) lithography machines cost $150M+ each, require custom vibration-dampened flooring; (4) Process yield optimization — achieving 70%+ yield (working chips per wafer) requires months of process tuning; these constraints make semiconductor manufacturing the most capital-intensive continuous-flow process in industry, with total capital costs exceeding chemical refineries
Semiconductor manufacturing uses a job shop process, which is why costs are high
Semiconductor fab construction costs are primarily driven by labor costs in the construction phase
explanation: Semiconductor fab as continuous process case study: TSMC's $20B fab in Arizona (under construction 2022–2025) is among the most capital-intensive facilities ever built. The continuous process logic: (1) Cannot make a single chip — must process full wafers of thousands of chips simultaneously; (2) Process chemistry requires uninterrupted sequences (400+ process steps over 3 months per wafer); (3) Yield management = profitability — a 1% yield improvement generates hundreds of millions in additional revenue. The capital intensity creates a massive barrier to entry: Intel, TSMC, Samsung — only 3 companies can make the most advanced chips — protected by the $15–20B capital investment required to participate.

question: A hospital treats patients through an emergency department (unpredictable, urgent variety), an elective surgery suite (scheduled, predictable procedures), and a chemotherapy infusion center (regular, scheduled, similar protocols). What process design differences should exist for each?
options: Hospitals should use a uniform process for all patient types to ensure fairness

Each unit requires a different process design matching the demand characteristics: ED (emergency dept) = job shop / project: unpredictable arrival, extreme variety of conditions, triage needed to prioritize by severity; must maintain idle capacity for surges; Elective surgery = scheduled batch: surgeries scheduled weeks ahead, predictable duration by procedure type, resource pooling (OR team, anesthesiologist) can be optimized around procedure type sequences; Chemo infusion = scheduled, near-continuous: predictable appointments, similar protocols across patients, chair utilization optimization, nearly standardized nursing protocols per drug type; forcing all three through the same process design would optimize none — the ED's process (designed for unpredictability) would be inefficient for infusion; the infusion process (optimized for regularity) would fail in an ED
Hospital operations management cannot be improved through process design since medicine is too complex
All hospital processes should mirror manufacturing processes since both are high-volume operations
explanation: Hospital process design: matching process design to demand characteristics. ED: 'make-to-order' with variable demand — must maintain spare capacity; triage sequences by urgency (not FIFO); highly cross-trained staff. Elective surgery: 'batch' with scheduled demand — block scheduling by surgeon/procedure type; same process design used 10 times a day for similar procedures enables efficiency; equipment standardization per procedure. Chemotherapy infusion: near-'continuous flow' — 6–8 hour infusions on predictable schedule; chair utilization management critical; protocols are highly standardized per drug/diagnosis combination. The inappropriate process design: hospitals using ED-style (full custom, unpredictable) processes for elective surgery (scheduled, predictable) wastes scheduling efficiency by not standardizing the schedulable.

question: A clothing manufacturer is deciding between: (A) continuing production in Vietnam (30-day lead time, $4/unit labor cost) versus (B) nearshoring to Mexico (7-day lead time, $9/unit labor cost). The CFO favors A; the operations head favors B. Who is likely correct given current market dynamics?
options: The CFO is always correct on cost decisions; lower labor cost always produces better outcomes

The operations head may be correct for fashion/trend-sensitive products: total cost analysis must include: (1) Inventory carrying cost — 30-day lead time requires 30 additional days of inventory-in-transit + safety stock ($4 × units × 0.25% per day holding cost); (2) Markdown risk — fashion products unsold at season end are marked down 40–60%; 30-day lead time means committing to designs 30 days earlier with less demand data = higher forecast error = higher markdown cost; (3) Order flexibility — 7-day lead time allows reordering trending styles and canceling slow ones; this demand-responsive advantage can offset $5/unit labor difference for trend-sensitive products; for commodity products (socks, basic t-shirts) with stable demand, Vietnam is clearly better
Labor cost is always the dominant factor in manufacturing location decisions
Lead time has no material impact on total cost in apparel manufacturing
explanation: Total cost of ownership in manufacturing location: the CFO's frame ($4 vs. $9 = $5/unit difference) is incomplete. The operations frame includes lead time costs: 30-day lead time → 30 extra days of inventory in transit × $4 unit value × financing cost; 30-day lead time → requires forecasting 60 days ahead (30 transit + 30 production) vs. 7-day lead time requires only 37 days ahead; additional 23 days of forecast horizon = 20–30% higher forecast error rate; in fashion, forecast error at season end = markdowns of 40–60% on unsold inventory. For a fashion retailer with 20% of seasonal inventory sold at 50% markdown: markdown cost per unit = 20% × 50% × retail price = potentially far more than $5/unit labor difference.

question: Spotify processes 600,000+ new song uploads daily, with automatic audio quality checking, metadata validation, content moderation, and distribution to 200+ music platforms. What process type enables this volume?
options: Spotify uses a job shop process, reviewing each song manually by specialist teams

Automated continuous/batch processing enabled by digital technology: music upload processing is near-identical for each song (same steps: format validation, audio quality analysis, metadata extraction, content scan, encoding for 5+ formats, distribution API calls); this standardization enables: fully automated pipelines processing each upload as a batch item with no manual intervention for clean uploads; algorithm-based content flagging for human review (only flagged items require job-shop-style handling); parallel processing across thousands of simultaneous uploads; the process design insight: digitization converts formerly labor-intensive custom processes (reviewing each song) into automated standard processes (algorithm checks same criteria for each upload at near-zero marginal cost)
Spotify reviews each of the 600,000 daily uploads individually
Content processing pipelines require human review for each item to ensure quality
explanation: Digital process type transformation: physical processes have different economics than digital processes. Physical job shop (review each book manuscript): 1 editor × 200 pages × 8 hours = 1 week per manuscript. Digital automated pipeline (grammar check + plagiarism detection + format validation): 200 pages processed in 3 seconds. The process type change (job shop → automated continuous) is enabled by digitization. Spotify, YouTube, App Store review (automated initial review + human review only for flagged items): the 'semi-continuous with exception handling' process design. Netflix: 600+ hours of new content added per day — fully automated processing pipelines with human curation teams reviewing flagged or acquisition content only.

question: A contract electronics manufacturer (like Foxconn) serves both Apple (making iPhones in astronomically high volumes) and a small medical device startup (making 5,000 specialized diagnostic devices per year). How must the process design differ for each customer?
options: Foxconn should use identical processes for both customers

iPhone production = mass production line: dedicated assembly lines for one product, automated where possible, extremely standardized (every iPhone assembled identically), high capital investment justified by volume (200M+ units/year), worker specialization on one step each, quality controlled by statistical sampling; Medical device = job shop or small batch: 5,000 units/year, high quality documentation requirements (FDA 21 CFR Part 820), potentially each unit individually serialized and traceable, less automation investment justified by volume, workers handling full assembly sequences; the contract manufacturer must organize separate operational areas with different process designs: attempting mass production processes on 5,000-unit medical devices creates mismatched economics and quality systems
Volume has no impact on the appropriate process design
Medical device manufacturing always requires higher technology equipment than consumer electronics
explanation: Contract manufacturing process type matching: Foxconn operates multiple factory segments for different clients. iPhone assembly: 200M+ units/year → dedicated automated lines, specialized single-task workers, statistical process control, 60-second cycle time targets. Medical device assembly: 5,000 units/year → general-purpose equipment (one piece of equipment used for multiple steps), complete documentation for each unit (Device History Records required by FDA), lot traceability (every component source recorded), full inspection on every unit (vs. statistical sampling). The process type determines: equipment investment (ROI at 200M units vs. 5,000 units), labor training (specialists vs. generalists), quality system (statistical control vs. 100% inspection), and documentation (ISO 13485 medical vs. ISO 9001 general manufacturing).

question: 'Cellular manufacturing' groups equipment into 'cells' where each cell can produce a family of similar products. A traditional plant has all lathes together, all mills together, all drills together. A cellular plant clusters one lathe, one mill, one drill, and one grinder into a cell that makes a specific part family. What operational benefits does cellular manufacturing create?
options: Cellular manufacturing creates no benefits compared to process-department layouts

Cellular manufacturing benefits vs. functional layout: (1) Reduced material travel: parts move within a 30-foot cell instead of across a 300-foot factory between department buildings; (2) Faster lead time: no queuing between departments — part stays in cell until complete; (3) Simplified scheduling: cell produces one family of parts, not all parts; (4) Quality feedback: the same worker(s) who perform all steps see downstream problems and can immediately trace to upstream causes; (5) Reduced WIP: no inter-department queues mean less work-in-process inventory; the trade-off: cells require more equipment (each cell needs its own lathe rather than sharing the lathe department), potentially lower machine utilization than pooled departments
Cellular manufacturing only benefits companies making exactly one type of product
Reducing material travel distance is not a meaningful source of operational improvement
explanation: Cellular manufacturing trade-offs: functional layout (all lathes together) has highest equipment utilization (all lathes share work) but terrible flow efficiency (parts travel miles through the factory, queuing at each department). Cellular layout: dedicated equipment per cell has lower individual machine utilization (the cell's lathe is only used for that cell's parts) but dramatically better flow (one-piece flow within cell, minimal queue). The Toyota connection: cellular manufacturing is part of lean manufacturing's one-piece flow objective. The quality benefit is underappreciated: when one worker handles multiple steps, they see if their earlier work causes problems in the next step — immediate quality feedback vs. functional layouts where the cause of a defect is 3 departments away.

question: E-commerce has evolved from: manual order picking → automated conveyor systems → robotic item picking → fully automated warehouses (Amazon Robotics). What does this process type evolution illustrate about the relationship between technology investment and volume thresholds?
options: Automation investments are equally beneficial at all volume levels

Technology investment justification is volume-dependent: manual picking: no capital cost, maximum flexibility, optimal at low volume/high variety (small warehouse, diverse SKUs); automated conveyors: $2–5M capital, eliminates walking labor, optimal at moderate volume with predictable paths; robotic picking (AMR robots): $10–50M investment, eliminates picking labor, needs 100,000+ daily picks to justify; fully automated dark warehouses (Amazon): $200M+ investment, runs 24/7 with no human workers after setup, only economical at millions of daily picks; the principle: each automation investment step has a minimum volume threshold to produce positive ROI; a company processing 500 orders/day should not use Amazon's warehouse design — the capital cost per order would be astronomical; a company processing 1M orders/day cannot afford to NOT automate
Higher automation levels are always better regardless of volume
Manual processes are always preferable to automation in warehouse operations
explanation: Process automation economics: fixed capital cost / units produced = automation cost per unit. At low volume: manual (high variable, low fixed cost) dominates. At high volume: automation (high fixed, near-zero variable cost) dominates. The breakeven calculation: $20M robot investment / $2/unit labor saving = 10M units to break even. At 1M units/year, breakeven = 10 years (marginal). At 5M units/year, breakeven = 2 years (excellent ROI). Amazon's dark warehouses are economically justified because Amazon processes 10M+ orders/day. A retailer processing 1,000 orders/day would lose money on the same investment. This is why process type selection must consider current AND projected volume — the right design for today's volume may be wrong at 5x volume.
